title: Did Google Just Fall Behind Again?, WWDC Preview, Anthropic Files To Go Public
summary: Alex Kantrowitz and M.G. Siegler discuss whether Google is falling behind OpenAI and Anthropic in AI development, particularly around coding agents and super-app ambitions. They explore how AI agents may reshape user interactions with web, email, apps, and browsers, potentially disadvantaging Google's search-dominant business model.
writeup: Alex Kantrowitz and MG Siegler (Big Technology Podcast, June 1) assess Google IO as a double miss: Gemini 2.5 Pro was not ready for the company's flagship developer conference, leaving Google without a competitive flagship model at the exact moment Anthropic and OpenAI had both shipped new ones, and without any serious entry in the agentic coding category where Claude Code and Codex are concentrating AI growth. The conversation traces that gap to a resource allocation choice: Google prioritized defending search, which has held up, but the cost appears to be missing the developer super-app moment that Anthropic's explosion to roughly $50B ARR has now made impossible to ignore.
key_points: Google shipped Gemini 2.5 Flash at IO but not the flagship Pro, which Sundar Pichai said explicitly on the Hard Fork podcast is still behind in coding AI, making IO feel like a conference held before the company was ready rather than at a moment of strength.

The agentic coding category (Claude Code, Codex) is now the central front in AI competition because those tools are seen as the beachhead for broader agentic use cases, and Sundar Pichai's public acknowledgment of the gap, combined with reports of Sergey Brin calling an all-hands-on-deck response, signals how seriously the deficit is viewed internally.

Google's search defense has worked: the company reports more searches than ever and the business has not collapsed as feared 18 months ago, but Siegler argues that focus on the core may have starved resource allocation toward the developer tooling frontier.

Anthropic approaching roughly $50B ARR and surpassing OpenAI in valuation caught the broader industry off guard because the value of the developer-and-coding-agent bet was not obvious to Microsoft, Google, or OpenAI until Anthropic proved it commercially.

The 'super app' label for tools like Codex (ChatGPT plus coding plus a browser unified in one environment) was initially dismissed as a misnomer, but after hearing Greg Brockman and Boris from Anthropic describe the vision, the hosts conclude the term is accurate: coding is the current entry point for a unified AI environment that will expand well beyond developers.

Annual developer conference cadences borrowed from Apple's model are mismatched to the pace of AI competition: a flagship model that is not ready in May is not a reason to hold IO anyway, but rather an argument for scheduling the event when the model is actually ready.

chapters.title: Gemini 2.5 Flash and the Missing Pro
